coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots and gold nanoparticles doped metal-organic frameworks as high-efficiency ECL emitters for monitoring of cell apoptosis
coredata.dc:creator: Liang, Guo-Xi
Zhao, Kai-Ren
He, Yu-Sheng
Liu, Zhi-Jun
Ye, Shu-Ying
Wang, Li
coredata.dc:description: Synthesis of highly efficient, cost-effective, Easy labeling electrochemiluminescence (ECL) luminescent materials is attractive in developing highly sensitive ECL sensors for many applications such as clinical diagnostics, biosecurity, and environmental monitoring. Herein, by doping carbon dots (CDots) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) into zeolitic imidazolate framework (ZIF-8), novel high-efficiency ECL emitters CDots@ZIF-8/AuNPs nanocomposites were prepared. Our experiment indicated that ZIF-8 in the nanocomposites not only act as carrier for ECL labels, but as an ECL accelerator to convert the CDots to CDots•–, causing the enhanced ECL intensity of CDots in K2S2O8 solution. AuNPs, which have excellent conductivity, were formed in the pores of ZIF-8 to further enhance the ECL signals. As a result, the nanocomposites showed a final about 7-fold ECL intensity as compared to the pure CDots. AuNPs in the nanocomposites could also provide a platform for effective attaching the biomolecular, making the nanocomposites ideally suited for building biosensor. As a proof of concept, the prepared CDots@ZIF-8/AuNPs was successfully applied in the rapid and simple ECL monitoring of caspase-3 activity during cell apoptosis, which gives impetus to the design of new ECL luminescent materials with ultimate applications in highly sensitive bioanalysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemiluminescence
Carbon dots
Zeolitic imidazolate framework
Gold nanoparticles
High-efficiency
Cell apoptosis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Reagents, apparatus, cell culture and treatments
Modification of streptavidin (SA) on CDots@ZIF-8/AuNPs signal nanoprobes
ECL behaviors of the CDots, CDots@ZIF-8 and CDots@ZIF-8/AuNPs
EIS characterization of the proposed biosensor
Measuring caspase-3 activity during cell apoptosis
Measuring caspase-3 activity in the presence of an inhibitor
Detection of caspase-3 activity with the biosensor
Selectivity, reproducibility, and stability of the biosensor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21707053 ) and Senior Talent Foundation of Jiangsu University (No. 10JDG052 , 10JDG038 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (a) blank ZIF-8, (b) CDots@ZIF-8, and (c) CDots@ZIF-8/AuNPs

(a) XRD patterns (inset: the enlarged part of CDots), (b) FTIR spectra, and (c) fluorescence spectra of nanocrystals (excitation at 330 nm, inset: photograph of nanocrystals under daylight and UV light illumination, from left to right are CDots, ZIF-8, CDot@ZIF-8, CDot@ZIF-8/AuNPs, respectively).

Cathodic CV and ECL of (a, a’) bare AuE, (b, b’) CDots|AuE, (c, c’) CDots@ZIF-8|AuE, (d, d’) CDots@ZIF-8/AuNPs|AuE, (e, e’) AuNPs|AuE and (f, f’) ZIF-8|AuE. Potential range: 0 to −2.0 V, scan rate: 100 mV/s. The voltage of the photomultiplier tube (PMT) was set at 800 V.

ECL curve of AuE in (a) ZIF-8, K2S2O8 solution and K2S2O8 + ZIF-8 solution, (b) ZIF-8 + PBS solution, CDots + PBS solution, CDots@ZIF-8 + PBS solution scanned from 0 to −2.0 V, and (c) ZIF-8 + K2S2O8 solution, CDots + K2S2O8 solution, CDots@ZIF-8 + K2S2O8 solution scanned from 0 to −1.5 V. (d) Possible mechanism of the enhanced ECL of ZIF-8 in the CDots@ZIF-8/K2S2O8 system.

Caspase-3 activity was analyzed from cell lysates using (a) based ECL biosensor and (b) a caspase-3 activity assay kit (colorimetric). K562 cells were treated with 5 μL/mL apoptosis inducerfor indicated time periods. Insert is the coccesponding ECL responses. The error bars represent the standard deviation of 3 times measurements. (c) ECL responses for caspase-3 activity in the presence and absence of the Ac-DEVD-CHO inhibitor for 30 min (n = 5). The active caspase-3 was obtained from apoptosis inducer-induced K562 cells.

(a) The linear regression curve of ΔECL values versus logarithm of caspase-3 concentrations: 0.1 pg/mL, 0.5 pg/mL, 1 pg/mL, 5 pg/mL, 2 pg/mL, 50 pg/mL, 100 pg/mL. (b) Selectivity experiment of the proposed ECL biosensor. Concentration of target (caspase-3), thrombin and trypsin: 10 pg/mL. (c) Reproducibility of the proposed ECL biosensor under same concentrations of target caspase-3 (10 pg/mL). Error bar represents SD (n = 3).
(A) Schematic illustration of CDots@ZIF-8/AuNPs-SA fabrication; (B) Schematic illustration of the CDots@ZIF-8/AuNPs-based ECL strategy for sensing caspase-3 activity.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots and gold nanoparticles doped metal-organic frameworks as high-efficiency ECL emitters for monitoring of cell apoptosis

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of the Environment and Safety Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of the Environment and Safety Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of the Environment and Safety Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of highly efficient, cost-effective, Easy labeling electrochemiluminescence (ECL) luminescent materials is attractive in developing highly sensitive ECL sensors for many applications such as clinical diagnostics, biosecurity, and environmental monitoring. Herein, by doping carbon dots (CDots) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) into zeolitic imidazolate framework (ZIF-8), novel high-efficiency ECL emitters CDots@ZIF-8/AuNPs nanocomposites were prepared. Our experiment indicated that ZIF-8 in the nanocomposites not only act as carrier for ECL labels, but as an ECL accelerator to convert the CDots to CDots•–, causing the enhanced ECL intensity of CDots in K2S2O8 solution. AuNPs, which have excellent conductivity, were formed in the pores of ZIF-8 to further enhance the ECL signals. As a result, the nanocomposites showed a final about 7-fold ECL intensity as compared to the pure CDots. AuNPs in the nanocomposites could also provide a platform for effective attaching the biomolecular, making the nanocomposites ideally suited for building biosensor. As a proof of concept, the prepared CDots@ZIF-8/AuNPs was successfully applied in the rapid and simple ECL monitoring of caspase-3 activity during cell apoptosis, which gives impetus to the design of new ECL luminescent materials with ultimate applications in highly sensitive bioanalysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) has attracted an avenue of interest in many fields such as biological analysis, environment monitoring, and pharmaceutical analysis due to the intrinsic merits of simplicity, economy, low background noise, high sensitivity, wide dynamic range and fast responsive [1]. However, the conventional ECL emitters such as Ru(bpy)3 2+, luminal and Cd-containing quantum dots have some limitations. For example, Ru(bpy)3 2+ is costly; luminol produce a weak ECL signal in neutral aqueous medium and Cd-containing quantum dots possess shortcomings of high toxicity and poor biocompatibility [2–5]. Hence, there is an urgent need to search for newly economic, high-efficiency and environmental-friendly ECL emitters. It should be noted that as recently emerging carbon-based materials, carbon dots (CDots) have attracted tremendous interest due to several characteristics, such as facile synthesis, water solubility, biocompatibility, and nontoxicity [6–11]. Nonetheless, the exploitation of CDots in ECL biosensors is less well developed because of the limitation of their low ECL intensity [5,12]. How to amplify the signal of CDots is the key issue in fabricating CDots-based ECL biosensors [5].

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), a kind of microporous material, with the merits of large surface area, regular pore structure, and adjustable aperture, have been widely used in many fields such as catalysis, separation, gas storage, bioanalysis and biomedical research [1]. They have also been utilizing as luminophores carriers for ECL signal amplification. However, almost all researched MOFs-based ECL materials have been limited to immobilize Ru(bpy)3 2+ and its derivatives into MOFs [13]. It is well known that Ru-complexes are not only expensive, but also have large steric hindrance, which makes them somewhat difficult to be loading. In addition, most MOFs inherently have poor conductivity that may prevent electrons to excite the luminophores, thereby limiting the utilization ratio of luminophores [14]. Besides that, the biosensing applications of MOFs-based luminophores are limited due to the lack of modification sites [1]. To further expand the ECL bio-applications of MOFs, it is highly desirable to develop a Ru complex-free MOFs with excellent ECL performance and easy biomolecule labeling property.

Caspase-3, is the mediator of apoptosis of mammalian cells and had been served as biomarker to monitor apoptosis [17,18]. Based on the aspartic acid residues (C-terminal) of caspase-3 could hydrolyze peptide bonds of tetra-peptide sequence of Asp-Glu-Val-Asp (DEVD) [19], a serious of biosensors was developed to detect Caspase-3, including fluorescence [20], colorimetric [21], electrochemical [22,23], surface enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) methods [19] and so on. Compared with those mentioned methods above, ECL shows many promising properties including low background emission, high sensitivity, and simple instrumentation required [24].

Herein, a novel ECL sensing platform of the caspase-3 activity monitoring was developed by the signal change of high-efficiency ECL emitters doping carbon dots (CDots) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) into zeolitic imidazolate framework (ZIF-8) (CDots@ZIF-8/AuNPs). The CDots@ZIF-8/AuNPs nanocomposites were synthesized, which exhibit greatly enhanced ECL emission compared to single CDots. The principle of the preparation is schematically illustrated in Scheme 1 A. We demonstrated that ZIF-8 can be used not only as a carrier, but also as an ECL accelerator to enhance the ECL emission of CDots in K2S2O8 solution. AuNPs possess superior electron transport capacity can accelerate electron-injecting rate in an ECL reaction [15], further enhance the ECL intensity of the hybrid nanomaterials. AuNPs are also an effective platform for the fabrication of biosensors due to their facile surface modifications, good biocompatibility, and inherent non-toxicity [16]. Subsequently, streptavidin was modified on the surface of CDots@ZIF-8/AuNPs by Au-S bonds between AuNPs and streptavidin. The streptavidin-labeled CDots@ZIF-8/AuNPs as the signal probe was bound to the biotinylated peptides through a specific biotin and streptavidin interaction. In the presence of caspase-3, the biotinylated peptides were cleaved and the biotin label was removed from electrode surface, which induced the decrease of ECL signals. Based on change of the enzyme-trigged ECL signal, a simple and sensitive ECL bioassay was developed for detection of caspase-3 activity. Since caspase-3 activity is the biomarker of cell apoptosis, this CDots@ZIF-8/AuNPs-based ECL biosensor could also be used to assess cell apoptosis. The specific construction process of the biosensor was displayed in Scheme 1B. This work shows that the use of AuNPs-functionalized ZIF-8 to enhance the ECL intensity of CDots is a feasible strategy for designing and manufacturing high-performance ECL materials, and thus may offer new opportunities to develop ultrasensitive ECL biosensors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reagents, apparatus, cell culture and treatments
ce:section.ce:para: Reagents, apparatus, cell culture and treatments are shown in the Supporting Material.

Initially, CDots were synthesized according to our published works [25]. CDots@ZIF-8 nanocomposites were synthesized following the method reported previously with some modification [26]. In the procedure, 5 mL of 2.46 mM Zn(NO3)2·6H2O methanol solution, 0.5 mL CDots aqueous solution and 5 mL of 19.76 mM 2-methylimidazole (Hmim) methanol solution were mixed in a vial. After stirred for 30 s, the mixture was kept static for 24 h at room temperature. The resultant yellow suspension was collected by centrifugation and washed several times with methanol, and the final obtained CDots@ZIF-8 was redispersed in 1 mL 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH 7.4) and stored at 4 °C for further use.
For the sake of comparison, ZIF-8 was also prepared with the similar procedures except that CDots were not added.

First, 2 mL of the prepared CDots@ZIF-8 dispersed solution was mixed with 12.5 μL of 1% HAuCl4, and then sonicated for 10 min, and stirred for 2 h to allow AuCl4- to be absorbed onto the surface of CDots@ZIF-8. Next, the reductant of NaBH4 (25 μL, 0.01 M) was quickly added and stirred for 20 min, and then sodium citrate solution (10 μL 0.01 M) was added dropwise and stirred for another 30 min. The excess NaBH4, sodium citrate, and unbound gold nanoparticles were removed upon centrifuging the solution at 9,000 rpm for 5 min. The resulted CDots@ZIF-8/AuNPs nanocomposites were finally resuspended in 1 mL of PBS (0.1 M, pH 7.4), and stored at 4 °C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modification of streptavidin (SA) on CDots@ZIF-8/AuNPs signal nanoprobes
ce:section.ce:para: The Streptavidin (SA) modified CDots@ZIF-8/AuNPs was prepared using previously reported protocols with some modification [27]. Briefly, 200 μL of SA (1.0 mg/mL) was added to 2 mL of pH-adjusted (pH 6.4, adjusted by 0.1 M K2CO3) CDots@ZIF-8/AuNPs solution, and then stirred in a 37 °C water bath for 4 h. The obtained CDots@ZIF-8/AuNPs-SA was centrifuged and resuspended in 1 mL of PBS (0.1 M, pH 7.4) containing 1% BSA to block non-specific binding sites, and stored at 4 °C for further use.

Prior to modification, gold electrode (AuE, 3 mm in diameter) was pretreated by piranha solution, 0.05 μm alumina slurry, washed with distilled water and electrochemical cleaning in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution to get a smooth and clear surface. Next, an aliquot of 10 μL of tris(2-carboxyethyl)-phosphine hydrochloride (TCEP)-activated biotin-peptide (5 mM) was dropped on the electrode and incubated at 4 °C overnight. The biotin-DEVD peptide-functionalized AuE (biotin-DEVD peptide|AuE) was further blocked with 1 mM 6-mercapto-1-hexanol (MCH) for 2 h. Then, the resulted electrodes were incubated in 100 μL of apoptosis cell lysates containing active caspase-3 at 37 °C for 1 h. After, the obtained electrode was immersed in the CDots@ZIF-8/AuNPs-SA solution for biotin-SA bioaffinity reaction at 37 °C for 2 h. Eventually, the non-chemisorbed species were washed away by PBS (0.1 M, pH 7.4).

ECL measurements of the electrodes were performed in 5 mL (0.1 M, pH 7.4) containing 0.1 M K2S2O8. The PMT was set at 1000 V and the applied potential was 0 to −2.0 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) with a scan rate of 100 mV/s unless stated.

The morphologies of the as-prepared nanomaterials were first researched by using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The CDs were prepared according to our published works, and the detailed TEM morphology of CDs also can be seen in our published works [25]. As shown in Fig. 1 a, Fig. 1b and Fig. 1c), the blank ZIF-8, CDots@ZIF-8 and CDots@ZIF-8/AuNPs all have the shape of MOF structure [28]. Furthermore, it can be seen that the incorporation of CDots significantly affects the size of the as-synthesized CDots@ZIF-8 nanocomposites. The formation of CDots@ZIF-8 may result from the coordination interactions between Zn2+ ions and the terminal carboxylic or N-containing groups on the CDots surface during the growth of ZIF-8 [26]. Therefore, the CDots could not only be encapsulated inside of ZIF-8, but also cover the surface of ZIF-8, which remarkably increased the immobilization amount of CDots.

XRD analysis was carried out to further characterize the crystal structure of the prepared nanomaterials. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the XRD patterns of CDots@ZIF-8 and CDots@ZIF-8/AuNPs are all similar to that of pure ZIF-8. For CDots@ZIF-8/AuNPs, a weak diffraction peak assigned to AuNPs (220) plane at about 66.42° is observed, indicating the successful introduction of AuNPs nanocrystals into the hetero-nanostructure. And no peaks related to CDots nanoparticles are observed in CDots@ZIF-8 and CDots@ZIF-8/AuNPs, which may be due to the masking effect of the strong nearby ZIF-8 peaks. FTIR spectra also confirmed the formation of CDots@ZIF-8/AuNPs. As shown in Fig. 2b, CDots@ZIF-8 shows both the absorption bands from ZIF-8 and the characteristic bands from CDots, such as the CO stretching vibration at 1650 cm−1 and the stretching vibration of imidazole units at in the range of 1350–1500 cm−1. And CDots@ZIF-8/AuNPs exhibit FTIR spectrum similar to CDots@ZIF-8 nanoparticles.

The presence of CDots and AuNPs in the respective samples was further proven by fluorescence spectrum (Fig. 2c). The CDots, CDots@ZIF-8 all exhibit green light under irradiation with a 365 nm ultraviolet (UV) lamp (inset of Fig. 2c), which is consistent with the emission peak located at ~500 nm in the fluorescence spectra. In contrast, blue fluorescent emission is observed for blank ZIF-8. For CDots@ZIF-8/AuNPs, nearly no fluorescence emission is observed owing to the high fluorescence quenching efficacy of AuNPs. These results further confirmed the CDots were encapsulated in ZIF-8 and AuNPs were successfully developed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ECL behaviors of the CDots, CDots@ZIF-8 and CDots@ZIF-8/AuNPs
ce:section.ce:para: Many reports indicated that the cathodic ECL of CDots was similar that of other nanoparticles, such as graphene quantum dots and Cd-based quantum dots given the use of a coreactant (K2S2O8) [5]. It is because S2O8 2− could be electrochemically reduced to a strong oxidizing intermediate, i.e., SO4•−, and then inject holes into the HOMO of the CDots to produce CDots+; the CDots immobilized on the electrode were reduced to the reduced species (CDots•−); then CDots•− could react with CDots+ to produce the excited states of CDots (CDots*), which decayed to the ground state with light emitting. Herein, we found that CDots@ZIF-8/AuNPs displays a greatly enhanced ECL compared to the pure CDots. In order to clarify the ECL mechanism of our CDots@ZIF-8/AuNPs, synchronously cyclic voltammetry (CV) and ECL responses of different modified AuE are presented in Fig. 3 .

From Fig. 3A, we can see that S2O8 2− displays a strong reduction current on all electrodes with a maximum response around −0.8 V, and for CDots@ZIF-8|AuE and CDots@ZIF-8/AuNPs|AuE, an obvious new reduction peak at −1.6 V is observed (curve c and d) ascribed to the electro-reduction of ZIF-8. From Fig. 3B, we can see that bare AuE has a negligible ECL signal (228 a.u.) in K2S2O8 solution (curve a’), and a strong ECL signal about 1965 a.u. of CDots|AuE was observed (curve b’). This behavior of CDots was consistent with the cathodic ECL of other nanoparticles in K2S2O8 solution, generally follows the coreactant ECL pathway [5]. It is noting that, for CDots@ZIF-8|AuE, the ECL intensity rise to 12,393 a.u. (curve c’), which is increased nearly 6 fold as that pure CDots, indicating ZIF-8 could boost the ECL signal of CDots. Finally, for CDots@ZIF-8/AuNPs|AuE, a further increased ECL of 14,283 a.u., ~7-fold to the CDots|AuE (curve d’), is observed attributing to the fact that AuNPs have the excellent conductivity and wonderful catalysts can improve the electron transfer efficiency, which decreases the potential barrier of ECL reduction [29]. Whereas, no ECL was observed for both ZIF-8 and AuNPs (curve e’ and f’).

To reveal the ECL enhancement mechanism of ZIF-8 on CDots/K2S2O8 system, ECL of ZIF-8, K2S2O8 + PBS, K2S2O8 + ZIF-8 + PBS, ZIF-8 + PBS, CDots + PBS, and CDots@ZIF-8 PBS were recorded. In Fig. 4 a, both of K2S2O8 with and without ZIF-8 show a maximum ECL signal about 1000 a.u., which is due to the emission of the singlet state oxygen (1 (O2)2*) in the two ECL systems. This also indicates that ZIF-8 cannot accelerate the S2O8 2− reduction [30]. In contrast, a comparison of the CDots + PBS, ZIF-8 + PBS, and CDots@ZIF-8 + PBS solution (Fig. 4b) reveals the ECL signal is significantly enhanced for the CDots@ZIF-8 + PBS, demonstrating the high efficiency of ZIF-8 in promoting the ECL emission of CDots. Our study also indicated that when the scan potential ranged from 0 to −1.5 V, ZIF-8 has little effect for CDots/K2S2O8 ECL emission (Fig. 4c). This may be attributed to the reason that ZIF-8 cannot be reduced to ZIF-8•– when the scan potential ranged from 0 to −1.5 V, thus cannot convert more CDots to CDots•–.

Finally, because ZIF-8 constructed from Hmim organic ligand and Zn2+, to further disclose the enhancement mechanism of ZIF-8, we investigated the effect of Hmim and Zn2+ on the cathodic ECL emission of CDots. As shown in Fig. S1, Hmim and Zn2+ exhibits no remarkable changes compared to free Hmim and Zn2+ (in solution). As Hmim and Zn2+ was found to have negligible effect on the ECL of CDots/ K2S2O8, the moderate 6-fold enhancement in the case of CDots@ZIF-8 may be caused by the porous structure of the MOF nanomaterials. According to these experimental phenomena, we could speculate that when the potential scanned lower than −1.6 V, ZIF-8 could act as an accelerator that interacted with CDots instead of K2S2O8 for enhancing the ECL intensity of CDots/K2S2O8 system. The possible ECL mechanisms are shown in Fig. 4d.

ce:section.ce:section-title: EIS characterization of the proposed biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: EIS measurements were performed in 5 mM K4[Fe(CN)6]/K3[Fe(CN)6] solution to characterize the changes of the modified electrodes. The results are shown in Fig. S2. Obviously, the diameters of the semicircle of the biotin-DEVD peptide|AuE, MCH/biotin-DEVD peptide|AuE, and CDots@ZIF-8/AuNPs-SA modified AuEs are much larger than that of bare AuE, and the diameters of are increased sequentially. This attributes to the inhibition effect of biotin-peptide, MCH and CDots@ZIF-8/AuNPs-SA on electron transfer. Finally, the semicircle diameter is decreased after the biosensor incubation with the cell lysis containing active caspase-3 because the modified peptides are cleaved by the caspase-3. All the results of the different modified electrodes implied that the biosensor for active caspase-3 was successfully constructed.

The prepared CDots@ZIF-8/AuNPs-SA could couple with the biotin-DEVD peptide on the electrode surface through SA-biotin interaction. Therefore, the effect of the SA and biotin reaction time on the ECL response was optimized due to the SA-biotin interaction is time-dependence. The results are shown in Fig. S3a. The signal intensities increase rapidly when the incubation time is increased from 0 to 60 min and then remain constant from 60 to120 min. Reaction time 60 min of SA-biotin was chosen for further studies.

Subsequently, the immersion time of the modified AuE into the active caspase-3 were optimized. As shown in Fig. S3b, a rapid decrease of the signal intensities is observed from 0 to 60 min and negligible change is observed after 60 min, indicating that the enzymatic reaction equilibrium between active caspase-3 and biotin-peptide could be achieved in 60 min. As a result, the incubation time with active caspase-3 was 60 min in the following experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measuring caspase-3 activity during cell apoptosis
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the potential application of the developed ECL method, K562 cells were treated with 5 μL/mL apoptosis inducer for different time periods, and the cells lysates were analyzed using this ECL biosensor. From Fig. 5 a, we can see that cells treated by apoptosis inducer show gradually ECL decrease with time prolonged, indicating the progressive caspase-3 activation. A minimum ECL value at 12 h is observed, more than 12 h, however, caspase-3 activity decrease. The results obtained are well in agreement with those obtained from the caspase-3 assay kit (Colorimetric) (Fig. 5b). Therefore, the proposed strategy could act as an effective assay for quantitatively monitoring the caspase-3 activity during cell apoptosis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measuring caspase-3 activity in the presence of an inhibitor
ce:section.ce:para: To test the applicability of the established biosensor to screen potential caspase-3 inhibitors, experiments were performed by analyzing the inhibition effect of Ac-DEVD-CHO. Fig. 5c displays the ECL responses of the proposed biosensor for caspase-3 samples with and without the inhibitor Ac-DEVD-CHO (10 μM). As expected at, significant inhibition of caspase-3 activity was observed for inhibitor containing sample. The corresponding relationship between the inhibition viability of Ac-DEVD-CHO for caspase-3 and the concentration of inhibitor was shown in Fig. S4 (IC50 = 5.9178 μM). These observations revealed that this biosensor has the potential for screening of novel small molecule inhibitors as therapeutic agents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of caspase-3 activity with the biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimum conditions, the proposed biosensor was used to quantitatively detect the different concentrations of the caspase-3. According to the change of the ECL signal “Δ ECL = E0 - E” (E0 is the initial ECL signal without caspase-3 and E is the ECL signal at different concentration with caspase-3) as the response signal for the quantitative detection of caspase-3 concentration. As shown in Fig. 6 a, there was a good linear relationship between the Δ ECL and the logarithm of caspase-3 concentration in the range from 0.1 pg/mL to 100 pg/mL and the limit of detection (LOD) was 0.03 pg/mL (S/N), which could achieve sensitive and stable detection of caspase-3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, reproducibility, and stability of the biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the selectivity of the established biosensor to caspase-3, thrombin and trypsin were selected as possible interference proteins of caspase-3 to test during the same concentration (10 pg/mL). As shown in Fig. 6b, the ECL response of thrombin and trypsin were approximate to the signal of blank. However, when the target protein and mixture were incubated with this biosensor, the ECL signal dramatically decreased. The results indicated the proposed biosensor has outstanding specificity for caspase-3. In addition, the reproducibility was investigated by constructing five same electrodes to immerse the same concentration of caspase-3 under the same condition (Fig. 6c), and the relative standard deviations (RSD) was 1.1%, indicating that the ECL biosensor has good reproducibility. The stability experiment of the established ECL biosensor was investigated by storing the prepared biosensor at 4 °C and measured every 10 days. The ECL response values from CDots@ZIF-8/AuNPs still kept at 92.6% and 91.2% of their initial values. The results shown that proposed biosensor has excellent stability.

In summary, the CDots@ZIF-8/AuNPs hybrid nanocrystals as high-efficiency ECL emitters were successfully fabricated. In the synthesis process, porous ZIF-8 could function as an excellent supporting matrix for the evenly distributed CDots and AuNPs. The introduction of ZIF-8 and AuNPs has been found to have a significant enhancement effect on the ECL intensity of CDots. Moreover, AuNPs formed in situ could be an effective platform for the fabrication of biosensors due to their easy labeling, good biocompatibility, and inherent non-toxicity. Considering the merits of CDots@ZIF-8/AuNPs, we proposed a CDots@ZIF-8/AuNPs-based ECL biosensor for monitoring caspase-3 activity. The results demonstrated that the CDots@ZIF-8/AuNPs-based ECL biosensor could be an effective assay for quantitatively monitoring the caspase-3 activity during cell apoptosis and has the potential for screening of novel small molecule inhibitors as therapeutic agents. CDots@ZIF-8/AuNPs can therefore be expected to environmentally friendly, sensitive, and unexpensive ECL emitters for fabricating biosensors.

Guo-Xi Liang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Supervision. Kai-Ren Zhao: Validation, Data curation, Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Yu-Sheng He: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Investigation. Zhi-Jun Liu: Software, Writing – review & editing. Shu-Ying Ye: Software, Writing – review & editing. Li Wang: Validation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21707053) and Senior Talent Foundation of Jiangsu University (No. 10JDG052, 10JDG038).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2021.106787.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Zhou, Y. Li, W. Wang, X. Tan, Z. Lu, H. Han, Metal-organic frameworks-based sensitive electrochemiluminescence biosensing. Biosens. Bioelectron. 164 (2020) 112332.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Pre-oxidation of gold nanoclusters results in a 66 % anodic electrochemiluminescence yield and drives mechanistic insights

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Peng, Z. Huang, Y. Sheng, X. Zhang, H. Deng, W. Chen, J. Liu, Pre-oxidation of gold nanoclusters results in a 66 % anodic electrochemiluminescence yield and drives mechanistic insights. Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 58 (2019) 11691-11694.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Combined spectroelectrochemical and simulated insights into the electrogenerated chemiluminescence coreactant mechanism

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.S. Danis, K.P. Potts, S.C. Perry, J. Mauzeroll, Combined spectroelectrochemical and simulated insights into the electrogenerated chemiluminescence coreactant mechanism. Anal. Chem. 90 (2018) 7377-7382.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemiluminescence emission from carbon quantum dot-sulfite coreactant system

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Dong, C. Chen, J. Lin, N. Zhou, Y. Chi, G. Chen, Electrochemiluminescence emission from carbon quantum dot-sulfite coreactant system. Carbon 56 (2013) 12-17.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Supersensitive detection of chlorinated phenols by multiple amplification electrochemiluminescence sensing based on carbon quantum dots/grapheme

Biosens. Bioelectron. 155 (2020) 112099.
